steps.title: Isolate the radical
steps.content:
Subtract $4$ from both sides before squaring.
$$\sqrt{2x+6}=x-1$$

steps.title: Solve the quadratic
steps.content:
Square both sides and factor the resulting equation.
$$2x+6=(x-1)^2$$
$$2x+6=x^2-2x+1$$
$$x^2-4x-5=0$$
$$(x-5)(x+1)=0$$
The candidates are $x=5$ and $x=-1$.

steps.title: Check the candidates
steps.content:
Squaring can introduce extraneous solutions, so test both candidates in the original equation.
$$\sqrt{2(5)+6}+4=\sqrt{16}+4=8$$
$$5+3=8$$
$$\sqrt{2(-1)+6}+4=\sqrt{4}+4=6$$
$$-1+3=2$$
Only $x=5$ works in the original equation, so the solution set is $\{5\}$. The answer is B.
